title: The Map of Parameter Space in Double Microwave Shielding
authors: Hubert J. Jóźwiak, Ian Stevenson, Sebastian Will, Tijs Karman
abstract: Double microwave shielding employs $σ^{+}$- and $π$-polarized microwave fields, tuned close to the lowest rotational transition, to engineer a long-range repulsive barrier between polar molecules. By preventing molecules from reaching the short range, this technique suppresses detrimental two-body losses and recently enabled the realization of molecular Bose-Einstein condensates and self-bound droplets. Yet, the optimal operating regimes of the shielding mechanism remain largely unexplored. Here, by leveraging the underlying universality of the scattering problem, we systematically map the four-dimensional microwave parameter space-spanned by the detunings and intensities of the two fields-to identify configurations that maximize both shielding efficiency and interaction tunability. We define optimal operating regimes as configurations that are strictly free of field-linked bound states while sufficiently suppressing two-body losses to exceed typical lifetimes of ultracold samples. In these regimes, we evaluate the elastic-to-inelastic collision ratios required for efficient evaporative cooling and explore the accessible tuning range of the effective dipolar interactions. Finally, to identify the best platforms for future quantum simulation experiments, we conduct a global survey of candidate molecular species under realistic field constraints. We identify heavy, strongly dipolar molecules as the most promising candidates, demonstrating that they can achieve extreme loss suppression alongside robust interaction tunability using only moderate field strengths.
summary: The paper maps the complex parameter space of double microwave shielding applied to polar molecules to suppress detrimental two-body collisions. By analyzing the interplay between $\sigma^+$ and $\pi$ fields, it identifies optimal operating regimes that maintain low loss rates while preserving tunable dipolar interactions. The findings strongly favor heavy, highly dipolar molecules for realizing robust quantum simulation platforms.
main_problem: To systematically map the four-dimensional microwave parameter space of double microwave shielding to identify optimal operating regimes that maximize both shielding efficiency and interaction tunability for ultracold polar molecules.
main_result: Double microwave shielding allows for significantly lower theoretical loss rates than single-field methods, identifying heavy, strongly dipolar molecules as the most promising platforms for future quantum simulation experiments under realistic field constraints.
method: The analysis uses coupled-channel scattering theory applied to a Hamiltonian including dipole-dipole interactions and two external microwave fields. Results are mapped across dimensionless parameter spaces using universal scaling laws.
model_or_system: The system involves ultracold polar molecules subjected to $\sigma^+$- and $\pi$-polarized microwave fields, which create a long-range repulsive barrier via double shielding. The model is described by coupled-channel scattering calculations incorporating rotational states and photon dressing.
key_observables: Two-body loss rate coefficient ($k_{	ext{loss}}$), elastic collision rate ($\kappa_{el}$), effective dipolar interaction tunability, and the ratio $\gamma = k_{el}/k_{loss}$.
important_parameters: Microwave detunings ($\Delta$) and intensities ($\Omega$), relative field strength ($\Omega_\sigma/\Omega_\pi$), and dimensionless parameters like $	ilde{ eta}$ and $	ilde{\Omega}$.
main_assumptions: The analysis assumes the low-temperature limit, utilizes universal scaling based on dipolar units, and must reconcile theoretical optima (requiring GHz fields) with experimentally feasible microwave field strengths ($\sim$ MHz).
figures_summary: Figures illustrate loss rate suppression vs. field strength for single/double shielding, map 2D cuts of the parameter space showing loss minima, and plot minimum achievable loss rates versus molecular mass or dipole moment.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical mechanism (double shielding) to developing the theoretical framework (Hamiltonian/scattering methods), systematically mapping the 4D parameter space, comparing single-field vs. double-field results, and finally constraining these findings by realistic experimental field limitations.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed quantum control blueprint for manipulating interactions in ultracold molecular gases, moving beyond simple BEC creation to actively engineer collision dynamics for advanced quantum simulation protocols.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Two-body loss rates as a function of the applied mi- crowave field strength for single (a) and double (b) microwave shielding. The universal dimensionless parameters, ˜Ωand ˜β, are shown on the bottom and left axes, while the correspond- ing physical quantities mapped for the NaCs molecule (ΩNaCs

FIG. 2. Limits of universality in double microwave shielding. Elastic (a, b) and loss (c, d) rate coefficients are shown for selected molecules for two selected double microwave shielding configurations. The universal baseline (dashed lines), obtained from NaCs calculations in the universal basis set (see text), is rescaled by the respective dipolar units for each species. Solid lines indicate coupled-channel calculations performed in an extended basis set including j = 2 rotational states for each molecule.

Figure 2 compares the elastic and loss rates computed using the universal basis set against exact, molecule- specific calculations for selected species with varying masses and permanent dipole moments. The universal baseline is shown alongside exact calculations performed independently for each molecule in an extended basis that explicitly includes the j = 2 states and uses the species- specific molecular parameters listed in Tab. I.

FIG. 3. Influence of elliptical microwave polarizations on com- puted loss rates as a function of the total Rabi frequency, Ωtot, for two selected field configurations. The inclusion of exper- imentally realistic ellipticities (see text for details) increases the baseline loss but preserves the qualitative shielding behav- ior and the location of optimal operating regimes. Results are computed for NaCs molecules.

FIG. 4. The lowest loss rate, expressed in terms of the dimen- sionless imaginary scattering length, ˜βopt = βopt/add, achiev- able for a fixed ratio of microwave parameters, ∆/Ω, under the constraint that no field-linked bound states are supported (red line, right vertical axis). The corresponding optimal Rabi frequency that minimizes the loss for each dimensionless con- figuration, ˜Ωopt = ℏΩ/Edd, is presented as a blue line with values shown on the left vertical axis. The circular markers and vertical dotted line indicate the global optimal operating point at ∆/Ω= 2.38.
